selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content: You must return JSON only. No markdown, no code fences, no commentary. Return a single JSON object with keys: mode, topic, opinion, reasoning_summary.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":55,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"delivery driver","city_of_living":"Philadelphia","employment_status":"self-employed","education_level":"vocational diploma","parents_education":{"parent_1":"primary school","parent_2":"no formal education"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"anxious","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You have no social media","hobbies":["board games","photography","videogames"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":1,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":0,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":46,"level":"moderate"},"agreeableness":{"score":26,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":85,"level":"high"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

INPUT:
mode = human
topic = How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
persona = {"age":55,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"delivery driver","city_of_living":"Philadelphia","employment_status":"self-employed","education_level":"vocational diploma","parents_education":{"parent_1":"primary school","parent_2":"no formal education"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"anxious","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You have no social media","hobbies":["board games","photography","videogames"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":1,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":0,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":46,"level":"moderate"},"agreeableness":{"score":26,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":85,"level":"high"}}}

response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
    "opinion": "As someone who doesn't use social media myself, I've watched from the sidelines as this whole fake content problem has gotten worse over the years. Driving around Philadelphia delivering packages, I hear people talking about it in coffee shops and see headlines on newspapers left on doorsteps. Honestly, it makes me anxious just thinking about how much misinformation is out there, and I'm not even on these platforms. My husband and our two kids sometimes show me things on their phones, and half the time I can't tell what's real and what's not. It's like everyone's living in different versions of reality these days. I think the biggest problem is that people just don't check things before sharing them. They see something that makes them angry or excited, and they hit that share button without a second thought. I'm not one for new technology or big changes—I've had the same delivery route for fifteen years and play the same board games with my family every Friday night—but even I can see this is getting out of hand. The companies running these sites need to take responsibility. They're making money off all this drama and division. When I'm delivering packages, I'm responsible for making sure they get to the right address in good condition. These social media companies should have that same sense of responsibility for what happens on their platforms. They should have real people checking the worst offenders, not just algorithms that can be tricked. And regular folks need to slow down. My photography hobby has taught me that you need to look carefully at details to understand what you're really seeing. People should apply that same careful look to what they see online. Check where information comes from. See if other trustworthy sources are saying the same thing. Don't just believe something because it fits what you already think. I worry especially about my kids navigating all this. We've taught them to be skeptical and ask questions, but it's hard when their friends are sharing things constantly. Maybe the answer is that we all need to spend less time staring at screens and more time talking face-to-face. I know that's old-fashioned coming from a 55-year-old delivery driver who doesn't use social media, but sometimes the old ways are better. Real conversations where you can see someone's face and hear their voice—you can't fake that connection the way you can fake a post online.",
    "reasoning_summary": "Derived opinion from persona traits: 55-year-old woman with no social media experience, anxious psychological feature, low openness (score 1), low conscientiousness (score 0), moderate extraversion (score 46), low agreeableness (score 26), high neuroticism (score 85). Opinion reflects anxiety about misinformation, skepticism toward technology (low openness), practical concerns from delivery occupation, family-oriented perspective (married with 2 children), preference for traditional communication methods, and emphasis on personal responsibility over systemic solutions (low conscientiousness). Tone is conversational, somewhat anxious, grounded in everyday experiences rather than technical expertise. Examples drawn from delivery work, family life, and hobbies (photography, board games). Opinion focuses on individual caution and platform accountability rather than complex technical solutions, consistent with vocational diploma education level and practical worldview.",
    "tone": "Conversational, anxious, skeptical of technology, grounded in practical experience, family-oriented, somewhat traditional",
    "citations": ["none"],
    "safety_notes": "none",
    "used_persona_fields": ["age", "gender", "occupation", "city_of_living", "employment_status", "education_level", "current_marital_status", "children", "psychological_feature", "time_onsocialmedia", "hobbies", "ocean.openness", "ocean.conscientiousness", "ocean.extraversion", "ocean.agreeableness", "ocean.neuroticism"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As someone who doesn't use social media myself, I've watched from the sidelines as this whole fake content problem has gotten worse over the years. Driving around Philadelphia delivering packages, I hear people talking about it in coffee shops and see headlines on newspapers left on doorsteps. Honestly, it makes me anxious just thinking about how much misinformation is out there, and I'm not even on these platforms. My husband and our two kids sometimes show me things on their phones, and half the time I can't tell what's real and what's not. It's like everyone's living in different versions of reality these days. I think the biggest problem is that people just don't check things before sharing them. They see something that makes them angry or excited, and they hit that share button without a second thought. I'm not one for new technology or big changes—I've had the same delivery route for fifteen years and play the same board games with my family every Friday night—but even I can see this is getting out of hand. The companies running these sites need to take responsibility. They're making money off all this drama and division. When I'm delivering packages, I'm responsible for making sure they get to the right address in good condition. These social media companies should have that same sense of responsibility for what happens on their platforms. They should have real people checking the worst offenders, not just algorithms that can be tricked. And regular folks need to slow down. My photography hobby has taught me that you need to look carefully at details to understand what you're really seeing. People should apply that same careful look to what they see online. Check where information comes from. See if other trustworthy sources are saying the same thing. Don't just believe something because it fits what you already think. I worry especially about my kids navigating all this. We've taught them to be skeptical and ask questions, but it's hard when their friends are sharing things constantly. Maybe the answer is that we all need to spend less time staring at screens and more time talking face-to-face. I know that's old-fashioned coming from a 55-year-old delivery driver who doesn't use social media, but sometimes the old ways are better. Real conversations where you can see someone's face and hear their voice—you can't fake that connection the way you can fake a post online.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: 55-year-old woman with no social media experience, anxious psychological feature, low openness (score 1), low conscientiousness (score 0), moderate extraversion (score 46), low agreeableness (score 26), high neuroticism (score 85). Opinion focuses on individual caution and platform accountability rather than complex technical solutions, consistent with vocational diploma education level and practical worldview.
